coredata.dc:title: A sensitive fluorescence nanoplatform for monitoring benzoyl peroxide in food using carbon dots coupled with glutathione capped gold nanoparticles as FRET probe
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Tingting
He, Haibo
Wang, Yishan
Si, Xiao Jing
Yan, Xiaoxia
Lei, Yunyi
Yang, Zhaosheng
Luo, Liqiang
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a sensitive fluorescence nanoplatform for benzoyl peroxide (BPO) detection is constructed from carbon dots (CDs) and glutathione capped gold nanoparticles (GSH-AuNPs). The fluorescence of CDs is first quenched due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect in the presence of GSH-AuNPs, and then effectively recovered when adding BPO. The detection mechanism lies in the aggregation of AuNPs in a high salt background due to oxidation of GSH caused by BPO, thus the amount of BPO is reflected by the variations of the recovered signals. The linear range and detection limit in this detection system is found to be 0.05–200 μM (R2 = 0.994) and 0.1 µg g−1 (3σ/K), respectively. Several possible interferents with high concentration show little influence on BPO detection. The proposed assay exhibits good performance for BPO determination in wheat flour and noodles, demonstrating its applicability for facile monitoring BPO additive amount in real foods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Glutathione capped gold nanoparticles
FRET
Benzoyl peroxide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent assay for BPO based on GSH-AuNPs and CDs
Characterization of CDs, AuNPs and GSH-AuNPs
Fluorescence assay for quantification of BPO
Selectivity of the fluorescent detection system
Determination of BPO in real sample

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21974085 , 61971274 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The characterizations of CDs : (A) the excitation and emission spectra and UV–vis absorption spectrum (inset images are the corresponding photographs of CDs under visible light and UV lamp light), (B) the HRTEM image, (C) DLS measurements, (D) FT-IR spectra.

The emission spectra of the GSH-AuNPs / CDs under different conditions. (1) 30 mM NaCl solution; (2) 2.5 % acetone in 30 mM NaCl solution; (3) 30 mM NaCl solution + 40 μM BPO; (4) 30 mM NaCl solution + 300 μM BPO; (5) 30 mM NaCl solution + 1000 μM BPO (inset images are the corresponding photographs).
TEM images and DLS measurements of GSH-AuNPs incubated with different concentrations of BPO: (A) 0 μM, (B) 40 μM, (C) 300 μM.

Effects of various concentrations of glutathione incubated with AuNPs (A), GSH-AuNPs concentration (B), temperature (C), NaCl concentration and (D) reaction time (E) on the fluorescent detection for BPO. (The detection system consisted of 25 μg mL−1 CDs, 1.3 mM GSH, 300 μL GSH-AuNPs and 30 mM NaCl at 70 °C for 45 min).

(A) The fluorescence spectra of the GSH-AuNPs/CDs detection system treated. with varying concentrations of BPO. (B) The fluorescence intensity variations (ΔF). versus the concentration of BPO (CBPO) in the range of 0.01–300 µM (inset image is the corresponding fluorescent photograph) and the calibration curve between ΔF and CBPO in the range of 0.05–200 µM.
Schematic illustration of BPO detection by using the GSH-AuNPs / CDs as nanoprobe.
Determination of BPO in wheat flour and noodle samples (n = 3).

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: No signal was detected in the original blank sample.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The amount of BPO added in 1 g wheat flour or noodle sample.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: 10 μL extracts was analyzed in the proposed detection system.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Results calculated according to the calibration curve as presented in Fig. 5B.

head.ce:title: A sensitive fluorescence nanoplatform for monitoring benzoyl peroxide in food using carbon dots coupled with glutathione capped gold nanoparticles as FRET probe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Shanghai University, Shanghai 200444, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food Science, Shanghai Business School, Shanghai 200235, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Food Science, Shanghai Business School, Shanghai 200235, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fluorescence of CDs was dramatically quenched in GSH-AuNPs solutions based on FRET, and then the fluorescent intensity was effectively recovered with the addition of BPO. The detection of BPO was achieved using GSH-AuNPs / CDs as nanoprobe accompanying with GSH-AuNPs from dispersion state to aggregation state.

Herein, a sensitive fluorescence nanoplatform for benzoyl peroxide (BPO) detection is constructed from carbon dots (CDs) and glutathione capped gold nanoparticles (GSH-AuNPs). The fluorescence of CDs is first quenched due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect in the presence of GSH-AuNPs, and then effectively recovered when adding BPO. The detection mechanism lies in the aggregation of AuNPs in a high salt background due to oxidation of GSH caused by BPO, thus the amount of BPO is reflected by the variations of the recovered signals. The linear range and detection limit in this detection system is found to be 0.05–200 μM (R2 = 0.994) and 0.1 µg g−1 (3σ/K), respectively. Several possible interferents with high concentration show little influence on BPO detection. The proposed assay exhibits good performance for BPO determination in wheat flour and noodles, demonstrating its applicability for facile monitoring BPO additive amount in real foods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Benzoyl peroxide (BPO) has been widely used as a food additive in the food industry due to its bleaching and sanitizing properties [1,2]. Nevertheless, it is worthwhile to note that excessive BPO in food would produce highly dangerous oxygen free radicals and probably translate into other harmful substances such as benzoic acid, biphenyl, and phenyl benzoate, which might further lead to tissue damage and a variety of diseases such as allergies, vertigo, vomitus and neurasthenia [3-5]. Worse still, researches have shown that BPO could be a potential tumor inducer and it might increase the risk of cancer [6–8]. To ensure the consumers are not exposed the potentially harmful effects from BPO, the maximum allowable content of BPO additive has been strictly regulated in various countries. In China, the national standard (GB2760-2007) for food additives stipulates that the maximum dosage of BPO is 60 mg kg−1, and the addition of BPO has been absolutely forbidden since 2011 [9]. The Codex Alimentarius Commission of United Nations determines that the allowed amount of BPO in flour products is below 75 mg kg−1 [10]. The U.S. Food and Drug Administration sets the maximum allowable concentration of BPO in flour products at 50 mg kg−1, and the Japanese Food Safety Administration regulates that the amount of BPO in flour products should not exceed 300 mg kg−1 [11,12]. Therefore, monitoring BPO for preventing the illegal addition of BPO in foods become an important issue in food safety analysis.

So far, chromatographic techniques including liquid chromatography (HPLC) [13], gas chromatography (GC) [14] and capillary electrophoresis (CE) [15] are generally recognized as authentication methods for BPO determination. However, these methods require complex separation steps with sophisticated operators. Electrochemical methods [16] and chemiluminescence [17] are also reported for BPO analysis, in which tedious procedures for the fabrication of modified electrodes or derivatives are needed. In recent years, metal nanomaterials based colorimetric [18-21] and fluorescent [22] probes have been explored and widely employed for analyzing various chemical species, which demonstrated the advantages of high sensitivity and selectivity, rapid and straightforward responses for real-time detection. Several gold nanomaterials based colorimetric assays have been applied for BPO determination lately [7,12,23], however, no gold nanomaterials based fluorescent probes for the detection of BPO have been reported up to now. As an alternative, it is also desirable for fabricating a reliable and sensitive fluorescence probe for BPO sensing by taking advantage of the surface plasmonic resonance (SPR) properties of gold nanomaterials.

Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) systems based on CDs and gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) is a powerful platform for complex samples analysis, in which the fluorescence intensity of CDs as the donor was weaken seriously due to a large overlap between the fluorescence emission bands of CDs and the absorption bands of metal nanomaterials as the adsorbent [24-27]. Gold nanomaterials are generally considered as adsorbents in a FRET system thanks to the adjustable SPR absorption peak upon the distance between inter-particles spacing and morphology change [28]. Liu et al. used nitrogen-doped CDs as fluorescent probes and AuNPs as quenchers to design a FRET fluorescence sensor of metallothionein in human urine samples [24]. Zhao et al. developed an on–off-on probe for the detection of NO2 − and enrofloxacin in actual water samples based on the FRET between green emission fluorescence CDs and in-situ formed AuNPs [27]. Hosseini et al. constructed a FRET system between sulfur-doped CDs and AuNPs decorated tungsten disulfide nanosheet for cysteine determination in human blood serum [29]. To the best of our knowledge, no one attempts to determine BPO residues in foods by applying the FRET system of CDs and AuNPs yet.

As a consequence, in this work, a novel fluorescence probe for BPO detection was fabricated by utilizing the FRET effect between CDs and glutathione-modified gold nanoparticles (GSH-GNPs). In theory, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched by dispersing in GSH-AuNPs solutions, and GSH on the surface of AuNPs would be oxidized to glutathione disulfide (GSSH) in the presence of BPO, thus the GSSH gradually detached from the surface of GNPs and led an aggregation of AuNPs when being exposed to a high-salt environment. The fluorescent intensity was then recovered with a red shift of SPR absorption peak of AuNPs, which could be measured and corresponded to BPO amount in the detection system. The proposed sensing assay has been successfully employed for BPO determination in wheat flour and noodles, demonstrating its applicability for evaluating the level of BPO in real foods.

Gold chloride trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O, 49.9%), citrate trisodium (99.8%), citric acid (99%), ethylenediamine (96%), glutathione (GSH, 99%), acetone (99%), and sodium chloride (99%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. Benzoyl peroxide (BPO, 99%) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. All the reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ) was used in all the experiments. The stock solution of BPO was prepared in acetone at a concentration of 12.30 mM, and the working standard solution was diluted with acetone to the desired concentration for experiments.

The fluorescence spectra were measured by a RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Japan). The UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired using a SP-2500 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shanghai Spectrum Instruments Co., Ltd, China). TEM (JEM-200CX, Japan Electron Optics Laboratory Co., Ltd) and HRTEM (JEM-2100F, Japan Electron Optics Laboratory Co., Ltd) were used to characterize the morphologies of synthesized nanomaterials. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) was performed on a Zetasizer Nano ZSE (Malvern Instruments Ltd, UK). FT-IR spectra were obtained using an iS50 FT-IR spectrometer (Nicolet Instrument Co., USA). All glass wares used in experiments were washed using aqua regia to avoid any possible contaminations, and then rinsed with ultrapure water and dried before use.

CDs were synthesized via a solvothermal method according to a previous report [30]. Briefly, citric acid (1.26 g) and ethylenediamine (1.6 mL) were dissolved in 30 mL ultrapure water. After transferring into a 50 mL teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave, the mixture was heated at 180 °C for 5 h. The autoclave was cooled down naturally to room temperature, and the obtained yellow solution was purified with a dialysis bag (MW 500) for 24 h. Finally, the samples were dried at 60 °C in a vacuum drying oven, and then an aqueous stock solution of CDs (10 mg mL−1) was prepared for further use.

Citrate-capped gold nanoparticles were prepared by the classical citrate reduction of HAuCl4 according to the reported method [31]. Briefly, 100 mL of 1 mM HAuCl4 solution was boiled and stirred vigorously in a three-necked flask, then 10 mL of 38.8 mM sodium citrate was quickly added. The mixture was reacted under boiling for an additional 15 min till the color of mixture turned into wine red for indicating the formation of AuNPs. The obtained solution was allowed to cool down to room temperature and then stored at 4 °C before use. And the adsorption spectrum of synthesized Au NPs solution was measured and then the original concentration of Au NPs in 1 mL deionized water was calculated according to the Lambert-Beer law: A = εbC. In which ε (M−1·cm−1) is the extinction coefficient, which is related to the core diameter of nanoparticles and can be calculated according to the equation (ln ε = k ln D + a, in which D is the core diameter of the nanoparticles, and k = 3.32111, a = 10.80505.) as reported by Liu et al. [32].

GSH-modified AuNPs were prepared by following Li’s method with some modifications [33]. Typically, 13 µL of 0.10 M GSH was added into an aqueous solution of 1000 µL AuNPs solution synthesized above, and the mixture was incubated for 60 min with shaking at ambient temperature. GSH was attached to the surface of AuNPs through the formation of Au-S bond, and the obtained GSH-AuNPs solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 30 min to remove the supernatant, and the final volume of GSH-AuNPs was adjusted to 1000 μL with ultrapure water for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent assay for BPO based on GSH-AuNPs and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In a batch experiment, 300 µL of the as-prepared GSH-AuNPs solution, 100 µL ultrapure water and 10 µL of different concentration of standard BPO acetone solutions were added in sequence into a 2.0 mL capped plastic centrifuge tube, and then the individual mixture solution was incubated for 45 min at 70 °C. After 6 µL of 2.0 M NaCl solution was added and well-mixed in a vortex mixer, the individual mixed solution was incubated for another 10 min at ambient temperature. Subsequently, 10 µL CDs solution (1.0 mg mL−1) was added under sonication for 30 s, respectively. The final concentrations of BPO were from 0.01 to 300 µM in this detection system. Then the respective sample solution was transferred into a quartz cell for fluorescence analysis at λex / λem = 359 nm / 444 nm. For quantification analysis, the fluorescence intensity of GSH-AuNPs / CDs mixture without adding BPO was recorded as a starting point for calculating the fluorescence recovery value (ΔF = F - F0).

Both wheat flour and noodle samples were obtained from local supermarket. Prior to analysis, 1.0 g wheat flour and noodle were fully dispersed in 1 mL acetone solution with known variable amounts of BPO (1, 10, 100 µg), respectively. The samples were extracted for 15 min under ultrasonication and vortexed for 2 min, and then centrifugated at 12000 rpm for 10 min to remove the insoluble substances. Then 10 µL of the collected supernatants were subjected to the fluorescence analysis following the procedures as described in section 2.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs, AuNPs and GSH-AuNPs
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the optical properties and surface chemical structure of the as-prepared nanomaterials, the UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra, HRTEM, DLS and FT-IR studies were conducted. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the CDs exhibited a broad absorption band with a typical absorption peak at around 350 nm, which was related to n-π* transitions of C = N and C = O groups. There was no visible fluorescence for CDS under sunlight, while a bright blue fluorescence emission was observed under UV light at 365 nm (Fig. 1A, inset). The CDs exhibited a narrow and symmetrical fluorescence spectrum with a strong emission peak at λ = 444 nm and excitation at λ = 359 nm, respectively. The HRTEM in Fig. 1B indicated the monodispersed and uniform spherical dots with 4 ∼ 6 nm in size distribution. DLS data also showed that the average hydrated particle size of CDs was (4.3 ± 1.5) nm (Fig. 1C). In addition, the FT-IR spectra in Fig. 1D displayed the stretching vibration peaks of functional groups including –OH (–NH) (3428 cm−1), C = O (1637 cm−1) and C-N (1238 cm−1), which were ascribed to amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs [34,35]. Also the synthesized AuNPs presented a uniform particle size of approximately 13.0 nm (Fig.S1-A), and showed a specific surface plasmon resonance peak at 521 nm (Fig.S1-B). And the concentration of the as-synthesized Au NPs was calculated to be 4.473 nM according to the experimental data of extinction maximum (λ = 521 nm) in our experiment. Moreover, the appearance of thiol (S-H) peak at 2518 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra (Fig.S2) confirmed the GSH-AuNPs have been successful synthesized.

The corresponding schematic diagram is shown in Scheme 1 . The CDs solution emitted bright blue luminescence under 365 nm UV-light irradiation. And the fluorescence of CDs was drastically quenched when encountering with a certain amount of GSH-AuNPs, however, the blue luminescence of CDS dramatically turned on with the addition of BPO. The quenching of fluorescence occurred due to an effective energy transfer between CDs donors and AuNPs acceptors, and GSH was oxidized to glutathione disulfide (GSSG) and then detached from the surface of AuNPs gradually with the addition of BPO, resulting in the aggregation of AuNPs under high concentration of NaCl. Since the FRET effect would be weakened with a red shift of the absorption spectra of AuNPs upon increasing the amount of BPO, the quantitative detection of BPO was facilitated by utilizing the fluorescence “off–on” phenomenon in such a nanosystem consisting of CDs and GSH-AuNPs.

The proposed detection mechanism was confirmed by the following experiments. As can be seen from Fig.S3(A), the emission spectrum of CDs overlapped with the absorption spectrum of GSH-AuNPs in the wavelength ranging from 400 to 600 nm, suggesting an efficient FRET process between the CDs and GSH-AuNPs. Moreover, it could be observed from Fig.S3(B), the average fluorescence lifetime of CDs decreased from 15.35 ns to 11.39 ns after being mixed with GSH-AuNPs, verifying that the fluorescence quenching was caused by the FRET effect in this nanosystem [29].

As shown in Fig. 2
, the fluorescence intensity of CDs almost extincted when being mixed with GSH-AuNPs (curve 1), however, the blue fluorescence emerged again and became brighter and brighter with an increased concentration of BPO (curve 3–5). Considering that BPO solution was prepared by acetone, the influence of acetone in this nanosystem was also investigated. It could be observed that the GSH-AuNPs / CD nanosystem exhibited a very weak fluorescence emission in NaCl aqueous solution containing 2.5% acetone, suggesting that a small amount of acetone in this nanosystem just contributed a little to recovering the fluorescence of CDs (curve 2). To investigate the influence of BPO on the micromorphology of this nanosystem, TEM images and DLS measurements of GSH-AuNPs incubated with different concentrations of BPO were performed. Fig. 3 displayed that there was only a very slight aggregation of GSH-AuNPs in the presence of acetone, and the average hydrated particle size of GSH-AuNPs was about 15 nm. However, the GSH-AuNPs aggregated severely when adding 40 µM and 300 µM BPO into the nanosystem, and the hydrated particle size of GSH-AuNPs increased to 25 nm and 45 nm, respectively. These results above well proved that the particle aggregation mainly resulted from the oxidation of BPO other than the presence of acetone. In this nanosystem, BPO would activate the dimerization of GSH and triggered interparticles couplings through the formation of GSH disulfides (GSSG), which was well consistent with the phenomenon reported by Li et al. [33]. Thus, the FRET effect between AuNPs and CDs decreased due to a less overlap between the red-shifted absorption spectra of GSSG-AuNPs and the fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs, which was reflected by an increase of the fluorescence recovery of CDs with the amount of BPO increased in this nanosystem.

To achieve desirable sensitivity of the proposed fluorescent assay, the influence of GSH content, GSH-AuNPs volume, NaCl concentration, the reaction temperature and incubation time on the fluorescence property of the GSH-AuNPs / CDs system were investigated.

Since the modifier GSH played an important role for stabilizing the AuNPs in this nanosystem, the concentration of GSH for the modification of AuNPs was optimized firstly. In this stage, the concentration of BPO was set at 40 µM for reacting with GSH-AuNPs with different concentrations of GSH, in which 10 µL of 10 mg L−1 CDs in the presence of 6 µL of 2.0 M NaCl as described in Section 2.5 was used as the fluorescence background.

As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence recovery value (ΔF) increased rapidly upon increasing the concentration of GSH, and a maximum ΔF value was obtained at 1.30 mM GSH. And the ΔF value was declining once the amount of GSH exceeded 1.30 mM, because an excess of GSH also induced the aggregation of AuNPs due to the formation of disulfides and resulted in a high fluorescence background of the detection system. Therefore, AuNPs modified by 1.30 mM GSH solution were used for further study.

As the FRET acceptors, the concentration of GSH-AuNPs nanoprobes played an important role in the sensing system. The fluorescence of CDs was quenched gradually when increasing the concentration of GSH-AuNPs in the detection system containing 25 μg mL−1 CDs and 30 mM NaCl in the absence of BPO. By comparing with the fluorescence intensity without BPO, the ΔF value in the presence of 40 μM BPO was found to increase with the concentration of GSH-AuNPs increased, and the highest ΔF was achieved when 300 μL of GSH-AuNPs was added as shown in Fig. 4B. The ΔF value decreased when the volume of GSH-AuNPs solution exceeded 300 μL, and this probably because an excess of GSH-AuNPs would act as the fluorescent quencher for CDs, thus the recovery of fluorescence was offset by the FRET effect resulting from dispersed GSH-AuNPs. Hence, 300 μL GSH-AuNPs was employed for the following studies.

Considering that a high temperature might promote the redox reaction rate between BPO and GSH, the temperature in the range of 30–90 °C was investigated in this detection system. It can be seen from Fig. 4C, elevating temperature to 70 °C caused a higher ΔF value with a weak FRET effect, implying that the oxidation of GSH by BPO was accelerated at high temperature and thus the aggregation of AuNPs occurred with GSH detaching from the surface of AuNPs. The ΔF value decreased when the temperature was over 70 °C, and it is reasonable because the reactive oxygen species released by BPO are unstable and easy to decompose into oxygen which would escape from the solution at very high temperature. Therefore, the detection of BPO was conducted at 70 °C to obtain high sensitivity.

As reported, the AuNPs dispersions were stable in acetone solvents but have a higher susceptibility to the effects of the addition of NaCl than purely aqueous solutions [36]. Therefore, the influence of NaCl solutions with a concentration varying from 10 to 50 mM in this detection system were explored. As demonstrated in Fig. 4D, the ΔF value reached a maximum under 30 mM NaCl solution, and then it decreased slightly with further increasing the amount of salt. In principle, the AuNPs tended to aggregate excessively in high salt solution without BPO and could generate a high fluorescence background due to a weak FRET effect. However, the fluorescence recovery through the oxidation of BPO is also induced by the aggregation of AuNPs in a high salt environment. The results suggested that there was a good compromise between the fluorescent quenching from FRET and the fluorescent enhancement from the aggregation of AuNPs when applying 30 mM NaCl in this detection system.

Fig. 4E reveals the incubation time required for this reaction. It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity increased sharply at the initial stage and then remained constant after reacting for 45 min, illustrating that BPO oxidation of GSH could be sufficiently completed within 45 min. As a result, the detection of BPO was conducted for 45 min at 70 °C in the nanosystem consisting of 25 µg mL−1 CDs, 3.15 nM GSH-AuNPs and 30 mM NaCl solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay for quantification of BPO
ce:section.ce:para: The optimized GSH-AuNPs / CDs system was used for quantitative analysis of BPO. A series of different concentrations of BPO (0.0004–12.30 mM) were added into the solution containing 25 μg mL−1 CDs, 3.15 nM GSH-AuNPs, and 30 mM NaCl at 70 °C for 45 min, and the fluorescence was enhanced upon increasing the concentration of BPO of the detection system as demonstrated in Fig. 5 A. The results corresponded to the transformation of a dispersion to aggregation state of GSH-AuNPs due to the formation of oxidized GSH by BPO. The linear regression analyses were performed by the fitting curve of the ΔF values versus the respect concentration of BPO. Good linearity with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.994 was found in the concentration ranging from 0.05 to 200 μM of BPO in the fluorescence detection system as shown in Fig. 5B. The limit of detection (3σ/K) was determined to be 0.01 μM in this detection system (i.e. 0.1 mg kg−1 in 10 μL sample solution), which is far below the regulated maximum limits (CA: 75 mg kg−1; FDA: 50 mg kg−1; JFSA: 300 mg kg−1; GB2760-2007: 60 mg kg−1), suggesting that the screening of commercial food with BPO additive exceeding the allowable standard could be easily implemented by using this nanosystem. By comparing the analytical performances of this designed assay and other reported methods [7,9,12-17,21,23] for BPO detection, the present fluorescence assay exhibited a distinguished sensitivity and a broader linear range as summarized in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the fluorescent detection system
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the selectivity of this proposed method for BPO detection, various substances (including K+, Na+, Cl−, Br−, H2PO4 −, SO4 2−, NO3 −, L-glycine, sucrose, and glucose) that might coexist in wheat flour and noodle were chosen as interferents, and the fluorescence responses towards these disturbing substances were examined under the optimum conditions. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity of the nanosystem was significantly recovered in the presence 40 μM BPO, however, none of the chosen interferents in the nanosystem could result an observable change of the fluorescence response even with a concentration of five folds of BPO, demonstrating that the as-proposed fluorescence nanosystem had a high selectivity towards BPO among common interfering substances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of BPO in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: The practical applicability of the proposed assay was validated by analyzing of BPO in common wheat flour and noodle samples according to the optimal procedures as described in section 2.6. A series of BPO standard solutions covering low, media and high concentration levels (1, 10, 100 µg g−1) were spiked into the samples, after treating with acetone, the collected extracts were analyzed using the GSH-AuNPs / CDs based detection system. No fluorescence enhancement could be observed and detected in the wheat flour and noodle samples without spiking BPO in our experiment, indicating that the content of BPO in both food samples was at least lower than the minimum value detected by our method (0.1 µg g−1). The recoveries were calculated by comparing the detected BPO concentration according to the calibration curve in Fig. 5B and the corresponding spiked BPO concentration. As presented in Table 1 , good recoveries in the range of 85.8% to 106.0% with acceptable RSDs (≤7.9) were obtained for BPO detection in both wheat flour and noodle samples, exhibiting that the precision and accuracy of the present GSH-AuNPs / CDs based fluorescence assay are desirable for routine monitoring of BPO in commercial food products.

In conclusion, we developed a novel and sensitive fluorescence assay for BPO sensing by adjusting the FRET effect between GSH-AuNPs and CDs. In this nanosystem, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was weakened, while it could be restored upon the aggregation of GSH-AuNPs in the presence of BPO. The proposed assay exhibited the advantages of wide linear range (0.05–200 µM), high sensitivity (0.01 µM) and superior selectivity towards BPO. By applying the good linear relationship between the fluorescence recovery value and the concentration of BPO, the quantitative analysis of BPO in wheat flour and noodle samples was successfully achieved with desirable accuracy and precision. The present work provided a sensitive and reliable method for monitoring BPO in commercial food products, which could be very applicable in food quality control.

Tingting Deng: Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Haibo He: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Software, Validation, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Yishan Wang: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Xiao Jing Si: Resources. Xiaoxia Yan: Writing – review & editing. Yunyi Lei: Resources. Zhaosheng Yang: Formal analysis. Liqiang Luo: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos.21974085, 61971274).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122552.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

Sci. Technol.
